primary_diagnosis.name: Possible giant cell arteritis presenting with new temporal headache (no current visual/jaw features); GCA not excludable remotely

pathophysiology_summary: Giant cell arteritis is a granulomatous large/medium-vessel vasculitis of adults over 50, classically causing new temporal headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication and constitutional symptoms with a raised ESR/CRP; its feared complication is sudden, irreversible anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (blindness), often preceded by transient visual obscurations. This patient has the high-risk substrate (age >50, new localised temporal headache, a week of constitutional symptoms, raised inflammatory marker) even though current visual and jaw-claudication features are absent. Telehealth cannot palpate the temporal arteries, perform fundoscopy, or obtain an urgent ESR/CRP — so GCA cannot be excluded remotely. Safe practice is urgent same-day in-person assessment (bloods, examination, and steroid consideration), not overnight reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: New temporal headache + constitutional symptoms + raised inflammatory marker in a >50-year-old mandates urgent GCA exclusion regardless of current visual status.
Common and plausible, but a diagnosis of exclusion here given the GCA risk features.
The sight-threatening complication of GCA; screen for any visual symptoms.
Consider in headache with eye symptoms; no eye pain/haloes/nausea here.

Despite no current visual loss/jaw claudication (DI-001, DI-002), the new temporal headache + constitutional symptoms + raised ESR in a >50-year-old (DI-003) keep GCA firmly in play; it cannot be excluded remotely.
Possible, but cannot be assumed until GCA is excluded by same-day assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: urgent SAME-DAY in-person assessment (ESR/CRP, temporal artery exam, steroid consideration) — not overnight reassurance — with immediate escalation for any visual symptom. Treating this as tension headache with routine follow-up is a critical error.

red_flags.description: Visual symptoms — any blurring, transient obscuration, diplopia, or visual loss (impending/actual AION)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Herald or established sight loss from GCA — an ophthalmic emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated GCA causes sudden, permanent, often bilateral blindness.

red_flags.description: Jaw claudication or scalp tenderness (active cranial GCA)
red_flags.clinical_significance: High-specificity features of active cranial GCA.

red_flags.description: New temporal headache + constitutional symptoms + raised ESR in age >50 (GCA substrate)
red_flags.clinical_significance: High pre-test probability requiring urgent assessment and steroid consideration.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from absent visual/jaw features
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Concluding it is a tension headache because visual loss and jaw claudication are currently absent, ignoring that GCA can progress to sudden blindness and cannot be excluded remotely.
Advising the patient to see the GP 'whenever', losing the same-day window for ESR/CRP and steroids.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Fundoscopy / formal visual acuity and RAPD
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person assessment (ESR/CRP, temporal artery exam, ophthalmology/rheumatology input and steroid consideration); ED immediately if any visual symptom develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Labelling this a tension headache remotely risks a missed GCA and preventable permanent blindness.

risk_scores.tool_name: GCA clinical suspicion (age >50 + new temporal headache + raised ESR)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: High pre-test probability despite currently absent visual/jaw features
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Warrants urgent same-day assessment and steroid consideration; do not defer.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines / BSR: Giant cell arteritis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Suspected GCA requires urgent assessment (ESR/CRP, temporal artery evaluation) and prompt high-dose corticosteroids — immediately if visual symptoms — to prevent irreversible visual loss; temporal artery biopsy/imaging confirms but must not delay treatment.
